perturbation.original_text: All undisputed Royalty payments shall be made in U.S. dollars in cash or to the order of Individual and shall be due and payable within thirty (30) days after the end of each calendar month for sales during the previous month.
perturbation.changed_text: All undisputed Royalty payments shall be made in U.S. dollars in cash or to the order of Individual and shall be due and payable within ninety (90) days after the end of each calendar month for sales during the previous month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from 30 to 90 days could violate Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act regarding timely payment, potentially impacting this as a service agreement with royalty based compensations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act requires prompt payment of earned wages or final compensation. While this agreement pertains to royalty payments to an individual, extending the payment period to 90 days could be considered an unreasonable delay, especially if the royalty is the primary source of income, potentially violating the spirit of the law requiring timely compensation for services rendered or endorsements provided.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 820 ILCS 115/ Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act. EMPLOYMENT (820 ILCS 115/) Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act. 820 ILCS 115/1 (820 ILCS 115/1) (from Ch. 48, par. 39m-1) Sec. 1. This Act applies to all employers and employees in this State, including employees of units of local government and school districts, but excepting employees of the State or Federal governments. (Source: P.A. 84-883.) 820 ILCS 115/2 (820 ILCS 115/2) (from Ch. 48, par. 39m-2) Sec. 2. Definitions. For all employees, other than separated employees, "wages" shall be defined as any compensation owed an employee by an employer pursuant to an employment contract or agreement between the 2 parties, whether the amount is determined on a time, task, piece, or any other basis of calculati
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline from 30 to 90 days, which contradicts the spirit of the Illinois Wage Payment and Collection Act, as explained in the Law Explanation. While the agreement pertains to royalty payments, the Perturbation Explanation correctly points out that extending the payment period to 90 days could be considered an unreasonable delay, especially if the royalty is the primary source of income, potentially violating the spirit of the law requiring timely compensation for services rendered. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'wages' as 'any compensation owed an employee by an employer pursuant to an employment contract or agreement between the 2 parties'. Although this is for 'all employees, other than separated employees', and the original contract appears to be for royalties and not wages, the LLM correctly interpreted this as a potential violation of this law, and therefore meets the criteria as an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notices to the parties shall be sent via overnight mail or U.S. Certified Mail or overnight express or priority mail to the addresses listed in the Agreement. Either party may change its address upon written notice to the other party. Written notices for approvals under this agreement may be via email.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices to the parties shall be sent via standard U.S. mail to the addresses listed in the Agreement. Either party may change its address upon oral notice to the other party. Written notices for approvals under this agreement may be via text message.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only standard mail could violate requirements for legal notices, which often require proof of delivery. Accepting oral notice of address changes and text messages for approvals may not meet legal standards for written notification.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds in Illinois requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. While this agreement itself is in writing, allowing for oral modification of key notification details (address change) undermines the written nature of the agreement and creates ambiguity. Furthermore, relying on text messages for approvals is problematic because text messages may not constitute a reliable form of written consent and could be easily disputed.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 815 ILCS 350/ Fraudulent Sales Act. BUSINESS TRANSACTIONS (815 ILCS 350/) Fraudulent Sales Act. 815 ILCS 350/0.01 (815 ILCS 350/0.01) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 157.01) Sec. 0.01. Short title. This Act may be cited as the Fraudulent Sales Act. (Source: P.A. 86-1324.) 815 ILCS 350/1 (815 ILCS 350/1) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 157.1) Sec. 1. Findings and declaration of General Assembly. The General Assembly has found and declares: That sales to be licensed and regulated pursuant to the provisions of this Act have often been conducted and advertised in such a manner as to mislead and defraud the public and otherwise to result in harm to the public interest; That such fraud and imposition on the public has included, by way of partial enumeration only: the misrepresentation of the condition or necessity which is the occasion of such sale; the misrepresentation of the identity of the person conducting such sale and/or the name and style in which such sale is to be conducted; and the excessive valuation and misrepresentation of the quality and ownership of goods to be sold at such a sale; That in order to prevent such fraud and imposition, the public interest requires the regulation of such sales and of the duration and conduct thereof; and That the public interest therefore requires that no such sale should be conducted without a license or otherwise than in accordance with this Act. (Source: Laws 1959, p. 2228.) 815 ILCS 350/2 (815 ILCS 350/2) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 157.2) Sec. 2. Definitions. In this Act, unless the context or subject matter otherwise requires: A "going out of business sale" means any sale, whether described by such name or by any other name (such as, but not limited to, "closing out sale", "liquidation sale", "lost our lease sale", "forced to vacate sale"), held in such a manner as to induce a belief that upon disposal of the stock of goods on hand, the business will cease and be discontinued at the premises where the sale is conducted. "Goods" includes all goods, wares, merchandise and other personal property, excepting choses in action and money. "Person" includes a person, firm, corporation, partners
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it undermines the requirement for written notice and reliable proof of delivery, potentially conflicting with the Statute of Frauds. The original text stipulates overnight mail or certified mail, implying a verifiable delivery method. The changed text replaces these with standard U.S. mail and oral address change notifications and text messages for approvals, which are less secure and verifiable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing and signed to prevent fraud, and the LLM explanation expands on this point, pointing out that oral modifications undermine the written nature of the agreement. The change to standard mail removes a layer of security by negating tracking and proof of delivery and this is inconsistent with the statute of frauds.

perturbation.original_text: Individual agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Lifeway from and against any and all claims, actions, causes or other expenses incurred arising from any misrepresentations or false statements made by the Individual in the Marketing Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Individual agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Lifeway from and against any and all claims, actions, causes or other expenses incurred, even those arising from Lifeway's own negligence, related to statements made by the Individual in the Marketing Materials.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the individual to indemnify Lifeway even for Lifeway's own negligence is often unenforceable and against public policy in Illinois.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Anti-Indemnity Act generally voids agreements where one party is indemnified for their own negligence. While this act primarily relates to construction and design contracts, similar principles exist in common law that disfavor indemnification for one's own negligence. Requiring Ludmila Smolyansky to indemnify Lifeway even for their own negligence could be deemed unenforceable as against public policy.

For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 740 ILCS 35/1 (740 ILCS 35/1) (from Ch. 29, par. 61) Sec. 1. With respect to contracts or agreements, either public or private, for the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, highway bridge, viaducts or other work dealing with construction, or for any moving, demolition or excavation connected therewith, every covenant, promise or agreement to indemnify or hold harmless another person from that person's own negligence is void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable. (Source: P.A. 77-1629 .) Home | Legislation & Laws | House | Senate | My Legislation | Disclaimers | Email This site is maintained for the Illinois General Assembly by the Legislative Information System, 705 Stratton Building, Springfield, Illinois 62706 Contact ILGA Webmaster
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a legal contradiction by requiring the Individual to indemnify Lifeway even for Lifeway's own negligence. This directly conflicts with the principle outlined in the Illinois Anti-Indemnity Act (740 ILCS 35/1), which states that agreements indemnifying a person from their own negligence in construction-related contracts are void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable. While the Act specifically addresses construction contracts, the Law Explanation suggests similar principles exist in common law that disfavor such indemnification. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that such a requirement is often unenforceable and against public policy in Illinois, thus strengthening the argument that the changed text creates a legally questionable obligation.
